content.assessment: Delaying tracking to 14+ would fundamentally restructure network geography and capacity by converting the currently bifurcated (primary/gimnázium) network into a unified lower-secondary phase. This is the proposal most directly implicating network-wide capacity and transport planning: the existing 880-school network is designed for proximity to generic secondary schools (F-live-school_network_planning-06), but a unified comprehensive phase at ages 10-14 would require redrawing catchment areas and potentially reallocating capacity away from concentrated urban gimnázium buildings (F-live-school_network_planning-05) toward broader, more geographically distributed provision. This is a large-scale network re-engineering task with significant transition risk.
opportunities: A unified comprehensive phase could resolve the geographic stratification driven by path-dependent school size and founding history (F-live-school_network_planning-05)
strengths: Addresses the root cause of geographic sorting identified in this lens: concentration of selective capacity in urban/Budapest nodes (F-live-school_network_planning-05) versus the network's general proximity design for ordinary secondary schools (F-live-school_network_planning-06)
Would eliminate the feeder-school dilution dynamic (F-live-school_network_planning-07) since no early transfer point would exist to trigger departures
threats: Historic urban/rural capacity asymmetry (F-live-school_network_planning-05) could reassert itself informally even after formal delay, if catchment boundaries are not carefully redrawn
Feeder-school staffing dilution (F-live-school_network_planning-07) dynamics could reappear in a different form if any residual selective options remain at the network edges
weaknesses: No transition-phasing evidence specific to the Hungarian network's capacity is present in this lens's findings, leaving unclear how the network would absorb converted catchment demand

Existing physical capacity stagnation (F-live-school_network_planning-03) suggests limited spare capacity exists to reabsorb students previously filtered into 6/8-year tracks without new construction or reallocation
